title: Does the cyclic group C_1093 admit a (1093,169,26)-difference set?
lean_gate_reason: No exact theorem packet exists to formalize. The blocker is mathematical: the current artifact does not justify promoting 13 to a usable multiplier for the exact cyclic row (1093,169,26).
micro_paper_assessment: The row itself remains a plausible micro-paper target if genuinely solved, but the current packet is not close: it breaks at the unsupported 13-multiplier premise and should be moved aside rather than widened.
family_affinity: Prime-order cyclic residual row with multiplier-orbit tools, but the present attempt fails at multiplier validation rather than at the orbit count itself.
generalization_signal: The reusable lesson is verifier-facing rather than theorem-facing: orbit-count obstructions in prime-order cyclic rows need an exact source-backed multiplier premise before any order computation matters.
strongest_honest_claim: The current packet does not settle the cyclic (1093,169,26) row. The bounded audit only supports the narrower statement that Baumert-Gordon still isolate the row as residual and that Theorem 3.2 justifies 11, not 13, as a 1093-multiplier on the present evidence, so the recorded 39-orbit contradiction is not an honest theorem.
paper_title_hint: Why the Present 13-Multiplier Packet Does Not Settle the Cyclic (1093,169,26) Difference-Set Row
candidate_theorem_slice: Audit-level slice only: under the canonical-source congruence criterion, 11 is a justified 1093-multiplier for (1093,169,26) because 13^3 ≡ 11 (mod 1093), but 13 is not justified because no power of 11 is congruent to 13 mod 1093.
